request: the turnstile doesn't work when I "put lever in turnstile" instead of "insert lever into turnstile"

messages.content: the turnstile doesn't work when I "put lever in turnstile" instead of "insert lever into turnstile"

results.name: The Gate
results.description: A worn stone archway marks the edge of the market. The stones are smooth from centuries of hands brushing them for luck on the way in — a tradition that predates any living vendor and which nobody has ever verified actually works. Beyond the arch, the noise hits you. A heavy brass turnstile blocks the path north, currently as immovable as the stones themselves. Nearby, a heap of junk has accumulated against the wall. A faded sign bolted to the archway reads TINKERMARKET in letters made from dozens of different metals.
results.secret: The sign letters are each made from a different dimensional metal. One letter — the K — is made from a metal nobody at the market can identify anymore. A void glass shard vibrates strongly near it. Tiv has been trying to buy the K for years.

results.name: Market Square, South End
results.description: The quieter end of the square, where the crowd thins near the gate. A wooden notice board bristles with pinned papers — requests, offers, warnings in scripts you can't read. A stone bench sits against a low wall, its surface polished by years of use. Nearby, a vendor tends a brass cart of roasted nuts that smell like cinnamon. The square opens up to the north.
results.secret: The nut vendor's cart runs on a heat source from the same dimension as the forge fuel. The sharper smell is a spice that mildly enhances perception for about ten minutes after eating — if a player buys and eats the nuts, descriptions of materials they examine should include subtle hints about the material's properties. Regulars know this and snack here before browsing.

results.name: Market Square
results.description: A broad open area where several paths converge between stalls. Awnings of mismatched fabric stretch overhead, filtering the light into patches of color. Vendors line the edges — some behind wooden counters, others squatting beside blankets spread with goods. Foot traffic flows in every direction. Someone is trying to deliver a crate to a stall that has moved since yesterday. Paths lead off to the east, west, north, and northeast.
results.secret: The square's layout shifts slowly over time — stalls migrate, paths widen or narrow. Long-time vendors claim the market rearranges itself to optimize foot traffic, though nobody can agree on whether this is literally true or just what happens when nobody's in charge.

results.name: Rawstock Row
results.description: A narrow lane between stalls piled high with raw materials. Bins of powders, coils of wire, jars of liquids in colors that shouldn't exist and in some cases are making a fairly convincing argument that they don't. The vendors here are specialists — they know what they're selling even if you don't. Stalls press in close on both sides, leaving just enough room to walk single file when two people try to pass, which they are constantly doing.
results.secret: The electric smell is dimensional bleed — so many materials from different dimensions packed together in a small space creates a faint charge. Experienced vendors use this as a quality indicator: if the charge is strong, they've got good stock.

results.name: Tiv's Materials
results.description: A cramped stall halfway down Rawstock Row, every surface crammed with containers. Bins of dust and powder line wooden shelves. Wire coils hang from hooks overhead. Sealed jars sit in rows, their contents shifting when you're not looking directly at them. Behind the counter, Tiv sorts through a crate with quick, translucent fingers, holding things up to the light before filing them into bins with practiced efficiency.
results.secret: Tiv organizes materials not by type but by dimensional compatibility — things that combine well are stored near each other. If a player asks Tiv about what goes with what, or examines which bins are next to each other, Tiv can hint at combinations. The jars that shift when you're not looking contain materials from a dimension with observer-dependent physics — staring at one long enough makes it hold still, and looking away lets it change.

results.name: The Open Forge
results.description: A communal workshop under a heavy canvas awning, open on three sides to the market. Workbenches are arranged in a rough semicircle around a central forge that glows with steady heat. Tools hang from racks on the support posts — hammers, tongs, clamps, things you don't have names for. The stone floor is scorched and stained. Grist moves between the benches, checking on work in progress and keeping the forge fed. A hand-painted sign reads: USE THE TOOLS. CLEAN UP AFTER. NO FIGHTING.
results.secret: The forge's dimensional fuel keeps the entire workshop at a stable temperature that's ideal for working most cross-dimensional materials. Crafting done here is subtly more reliable than crafting done elsewhere in the market — materials bind more cleanly, edges seal better. Grist knows this and doesn't advertise it.

results.name: Oddments Alley
results.description: A side passage off the square, narrower and quieter than the main market. The stalls here are smaller, their wares stranger — things that don't fit neatly into categories. A vendor displays what might be musical instruments or surgical tools. Another sells jars of light in different colors. The noise from the main square fades quickly here. Things cost differently.
results.secret: Oddments Alley attracts vendors selling things from dimensions that have less overlap with the market's usual trading partners. The goods here are harder to categorize because the dimensional physics they come from are more foreign. This is where the Fair Exchange begins — the transition from money to other forms of payment.

results.name: The Fair Exchange
results.description: An open court beyond Oddments Alley, paved in stone that's been swept clean. The stalls here are more permanent — wooden frames with cloth walls, some with actual doors. A sign at the entrance reads ALL PRICES POSTED in letters that look like they mean it. The vendors sit behind their counters with the patient attention of people who know exactly what they want and are prepared to wait for it.
results.secret: The prices are posted, but not in money. One stall's sign reads: "One hour of your undivided attention." Another: "A favor, to be named." The signs are binding — not magically, but by the market's custom, which here amounts to the same thing. Vendors who don't honor posted terms lose their pitch.

results.name: The Broker's Nook
results.description: A small stall at the back of the Fair Exchange, walled on three sides by shelves of ledgers. Madame Wick sits behind a desk, writing in one ledger while consulting two others. A brass scale sits on the desk beside her, though what it weighs isn't obvious — one side holds a feather, the other side holds nothing, and they balance perfectly.
results.secret: Madame Wick brokers exchanges between parties who can't agree on terms. The scale measures equivalence — not weight, but value as perceived by both parties. When both sides of a deal feel fair, the scale balances. She charges a commission: she keeps one detail from each transaction, which she records in her ledgers. She knows more about the market's dealings than anyone.

results.name: The Rendering Yards
results.description: The market opens up and gets louder. Canvas awnings give way to corrugated metal roofing. The ground underfoot changes from cobblestone to scored iron plate. Somewhere ahead, something large is being hit repeatedly with something larger. A hand-lettered sign bolted to a post reads RENDERING YARDS with an arrow. Below it, someone has added WATCH YOUR STEP, which given the state of the floor is less advice than understatement.
results.secret: The iron plates on the ground are dimensional scrap — failed castings and offcuts from the workshops ahead. They've been trampled flat by foot traffic over years. Some still carry faint properties from their source dimensions: one plate near the entrance is always slightly warm, another hums if you stand on it.

results.name: The Smelting Bay
results.description: A wide, open workspace dominated by three crucibles set into a brick platform. Each one glows a different color — orange, blue-white, and something that isn't a color so much as a headache. Racks of molds line the walls. A four-armed worker pulls a crucible from the heat, pours a thin stream of molten something into a mold, replaces the crucible, and picks up a sandwich, all without pausing. The air tastes like hot pennies.
results.secret: The three crucibles run at different dimensional temperatures — not just hot and hotter, but hot in different ways. Some materials only melt in the blue-white crucible. Others need the third one, which operates at a temperature that doesn't map to normal physics. The four-armed worker, Kelp, has been here long enough to know which crucible takes what by the sound a material makes when you tap it.

results.name: The Nut Cart
results.description: A brass cart on iron wheels, tended by a vendor with too many elbows. The cart's surface is a griddle heated from below by something that glows without flame. Nuts of various sizes roast in neat rows, each type in its own lane. A hand-lettered menu board lists names you can't pronounce and prices you can.
results.secret: The vendor, Pell, roasts nuts from seven different dimensions. The spice blend includes a perception-enhancing compound — eating the nuts sharpens your senses for about ten minutes. Pell knows this and considers it a selling point, though they don't advertise it. The cart itself has been in the same family for three generations, which in Pell's species is about forty years.

results.name: Wire & Filament
results.description: A stall draped in coils. Wire of every gauge and material hangs from hooks, loops from pegs, and pools in baskets. The vendor sits in the middle of it all like a spider, splicing two wires together with a tool that looks like pliers crossed with a tuning fork. A sign reads WIRE & FILAMENT — CUT TO LENGTH.
results.secret: The vendor, Spool, can hear the resonant frequency of any wire by running a fingernail along it. They stock coppervine alongside conventional wire, and they know that coppervine spliced to certain other wires creates a conductor that carries vibration and electricity simultaneously. They don't mention this unless asked, because the combination is expensive.

results.name: Food Row
results.description: A stretch of the market given over to food stalls. Steam rises from griddles and pots. A vendor fries something flat and round on a stone heated from beneath. Another serves bowls of broth from a vat tall enough to stand in. The smells change every few steps. A bench along one wall is occupied by vendors on their breaks, eating quickly.
results.secret: Food Row serves as the market's informal commons — vendors from different sections come here to eat and gossip. Conversations overheard here hint at what's selling, what's scarce, and who's been buying in bulk. The broth vendor, who has been here longer than anyone, knows every regular by name and order.

results.name: The Lens Grinder's Bench
results.description: A workbench under a stretched canvas awning, its surface covered in glass dust. Racks of lenses in various stages of completion line the back wall — some clear, some tinted, one that appears to show a different room when you look through it. The grinder works with slow, deliberate strokes, pausing between each to hold the lens up and squint through it.
results.secret: The lens grinder, Vero, makes lenses from dimensional glass — not just void glass, but glass from several dimensions with different optical properties. A lens that combines two types of dimensional glass can reveal things invisible to the naked eye: material resonances, dimensional stress fractures, the faint glow of active crafting bonds. Vero doesn't sell these openly because the last time they did, someone used one to cheat at a materials auction.

results.name: The Bindery
results.description: A stall hung with sheets of pressed fiber in dozens of colors and textures. Some are paper, some are cloth, some are something else — one sheet ripples faintly as if breathing. The vendor sits cross-legged on a cushion behind a low table, stitching pages into a cover with thread that glows faintly at the needle.
results.secret: The vendor, Quill, binds books and ledgers, but the real trade is in binding agreements. Write a deal on Quill's paper, in Quill's ink, and the words hold — not magically, but because Quill's materials come from a dimension where written language has mild structural properties. A contract written here is slightly harder to break than one written elsewhere. Quill charges in stories: tell them something true that happened to you, and they'll bind a page for you.

results.name: The Press House
results.description: A shed-sized machine dominates this workspace — a dimensional press that flattens raw material into sheets. The operator feeds chunks of something dark into the top and cranks a wheel the size of a dinner table. Sheets of material emerge from the bottom, thin enough to see through and warm to the touch. The floor is littered with offcuts and failed runs.
results.secret: The press can flatten almost any dimensional material into sheet form, which changes its properties — bulk void glass is brittle, but pressed void glass is flexible and can be cut with scissors. The operator, Drum, will press your materials for a fee. Pressed materials combine differently than their bulk forms, opening up crafting possibilities that don't exist otherwise.

results.name: The Brew Nook
results.description: A low wooden counter serves as the heart of this stall, where steam rises in lazy violet curls from ceramic pots. Tall stools are occupied by weary artisans speaking in low, modulated tones. A chalkboard lists beverages by their viscosity rather than their flavor. The floor is covered in dried reeds that muffle the noise of the main market.
results.secret: The reeds on the floor conceal a loose floorboard where vendors hide 'grey market' items for quick hand-offs. The tea's light frequency can be used to reveal certain invisible inks found on market ledgers.
scenery: A chalkboard listing prices for 'Dense Violet', 'Sheer Amber', and 'Opaque Grey'.
A row of mismatched ceramic mugs with thumb-rests shaped for different hand structures.
A small stove made of blue tile that hums at a low frequency.

ai.prompt: The atmosphere is hushed and contemplative. Focus on the sensory details of the strange liquids and the quiet commerce of information.
ai.conversationPrompt: The patrons here are mostly off-duty market vendors discussing the fluctuating price of raw materials and market gossip. They are polite but tired.

results.name: Lithic Shaper Passage
results.description: The purple steam of the brew shops thins, replaced by a crisp draft that smells of wet slate and precision. Tall, narrow walls of roughly hewn basalt press inward, their surfaces covered in fine white dust from the workshops ahead. A gutter runs down the center of the path, carrying a slow-moving sludge of gray particulate. This is a quiet transition, according to those who find the sound of chisels more relaxing than conversation.
results.secret: The white dust on the walls is actually a byproduct of 'ghost-rock' carving; it glows faintly in total darkness. If the player smears the dust on their eyes, they gain temporary thermal vision.
scenery: A steady trickle of gray runoff in the center gutter.
Basalt walls etched with geometric testing patterns.
A discarded sanding block made of rough volcanic glass.

ai.prompt: The atmosphere is industrial but subdued. Focus on the physical labor of stone-shaping and the properties of different mineral dusts.

results.name: The Dust-Mason Ateliers
results.description: A row of low-slung, open-fronted sheds lines this wide stone terrace, each echoing with the rhythmic tap-tap of hammers on granite. Thick slabs of variegated marble and porous pumice are stacked like cordwood between the workbenches. The air is so saturated with mineral dust that it feels heavy, providing a tactile quality to the silence between strikes. The masons here are very busy, which is why they haven't noticed you are standing in their light.
results.secret: The dust in the air is actually a mix of different stone types; if the player has a magnetic item, they can pull specific metallic filings out of the air to collect them as raw material.
scenery: A stack of blue-veined marble slabs leaning against a pillar.
A heavy wooden workbench scarred by decades of chisel slips.
A bucket of grey sludge used for cooling cutting blades.

ai.prompt: The atmosphere is industrial and productive. Vendors here focus on stone types, weights, and masonry tools. The masons are indifferent to travelers unless they have interesting materials to shape.

results.name: Hydro-Abrasive Gallery
results.description: A constant hiss dominates this narrow corridor where needle-thin jets of water pierce the silence. Massive slabs of igneous rock sit on sliding steel rails, slowly creeping through the path of the cutters to be bisected with surgical precision. The floor is perpetually slick with a slurry of water and garnet sand, which the masons seem to consider a decorative choice.
results.secret: The floor drains contain large amounts of settled garnet sand. If a player searches the drains, they can recover high-quality abrasive material for crafting or for use with the lance.
scenery: The smell of wet stone and ozone.
Thin, vibrating lines of water that look solid until they hit stone.
Piles of garnet sand in ceramic bins.

ai.prompt: The environment is loud and wet. Masons here are technical and precise, more like engineers than artists. The water pressure is lethal if touched directly.

results.name: Into the Market
results.description: The archway opens onto the south end of the market square. The noise and bustle grow louder ahead.

results.name: stuck turnstile
results.description: A heavy brass turnstile that guards the entrance to the market proper. It looks like it should rotate, but the socket for the operating lever is empty. This is apparently the market's way of ensuring only people with basic mechanical skills or a lot of patience get inside.
results.secret: The turnstile is part of a series of security measures that haven't been maintained since the last Great Reorganization. It requires a simple lever to function. Any long, sturdy rod will do, but the one in the junk pile is the most convenient.

results.name: heap of junk
results.description: A heap of discarded dimensional shipping crates and bent metal. It's the kind of pile that accumulates when nobody is quite sure if something is trash or a rare component. Someone has thoughtfully added a sign that says NOT FOR SCAVENGING, which has been largely ignored. A rusty lever pokes out from the mess.
results.secret: The pile contains a few useful bits of scrap. This is a common occurrence at the market — one person's junk is another's critical spare part.

results.name: rusty lever
results.description: A solid iron bar with a handle at one end. It looks like it was once part of a larger machine. It's heavy, rusted, and exactly the kind of thing you'd want if you needed to move something very stubborn.
results.secret: This lever was originally the manual override for a pressure valve in the Rendering Yards. It's now perfectly sized to fit the socket of the gate turnstile. It can also be used as a crude club, though the market has rules against that sort of thing.

results.name: Kip
results.description: An elderly vendor with skin like parchment who sits on a stool by the gate, watching the traffic with an expression of profound boredom. He wears a vest with too many pockets and a hat that might have once been a different color. He seems to be the unofficial gatekeeper, or at least the person most likely to complain if you do something wrong.
results.secret: Kip has been sitting here for forty years. He doesn't work for the market; he just likes to watch the people. He knows the turnstile is broken and enjoys watching newcomers figure it out. If asked, he'll mention that 'the last fellow who tried to force it ended up with a very sore shoulder and a very intact turnstile.'

results.name: stuck turnstile
results.description: A heavy brass turnstile that guards the entrance to the market proper. It looks like it should rotate, but the socket for the operating lever is empty. This is apparently the market's way of ensuring only people with basic mechanical skills or a lot of patience get inside.
results.secret: The turnstile is part of a series of security measures that haven't been maintained since the last Great Reorganization. It requires a simple lever to function. Any long, sturdy rod will do, but the one in the junk pile is the most convenient.

ai.prompt:
This is the main commercial zone of Tinkermarket. Bustle, density, commerce.

The market is packed. Stalls press against each other. Vendors call out to passersby. Crates are being unloaded from somewhere that doesn't seem to connect to any street. Three species you've never seen before are haggling over something in a jar.

Descriptions should include incidental background activity — transactions happening nearby, deliveries arriving, a vendor chasing a customer who forgot their change. The market is alive and indifferent to the player.

Materials and biology described with specificity. A vendor's skin is chitinous and blue-black, not "alien-looking." A powder is white and faintly magnetic, not "mysterious." Strange things treated as the texture of daily life, not as spectacle.
ai.conversationPrompt: NPCs here are working. They talk while sorting stock, making change, or hauling crates. They're direct — they want to know what you're buying, selling, or getting out of the way for. Conversation is transactional but not unfriendly. They have opinions about their competitors, their stock, and your taste.

ai.prompt:
The quieter, stranger side of the market. What you pay here isn't always money.

Vendors in the Fair Exchange trade in favors, time, obligations, memories, skill, attention. The price is always specific and always exactly what they asked for. A vendor might want three hours of your afternoon. Another wants you to carry a message. Another wants a skill you have — not to learn it, just to hold it for a while.

People here are more careful than in the main market. They read the fine print. They negotiate slowly. Not because it's dangerous — the deals are fair. Fairness here is precise and literal.

The humor is darker. The asides note the small print. A sign reads "All Sales Final" and means it in ways you haven't considered yet.

The feel draws from Spirited Away's bathhouse economy, Stardust's market, Gaiman's London Below. Strange beings engaged in mundane commerce where the currency is stranger than the goods.
ai.conversationPrompt: NPCs here speak carefully and precisely. They state terms, not prices. They listen more than they talk. They're polite in a way that makes you want to read the fine print. They don't rush you — they have all the time in the world, which may literally be what they're selling.

ai.prompt:
The industrial back end of the market. Heavier, dirtier, louder.

This is where raw materials are processed — smelted, refined, broken down, recombined. Dimensional engines hiss and clank. Vats of something bubble. A press the size of a shed flattens material into sheets so thin they're translucent.

The people here work with their hands. They're not shopkeepers. You buy from them the way you buy from a blacksmith: you wait until they're done with what they're doing, and you ask. They'll sell to you, but you're not their priority.

Descriptions are dense and physical. The texture of grime and labor. Strange biology treated as workplace reality — a worker with four arms isn't remarkable; what's remarkable is that they still can't find a wrench. Mieville's voice: specificity of the industrial, the weight and heat and smell of materials being transformed.

More dangerous than the main market. Materials in unstable states, heat, fumes, moving parts. The asides note safety hazards that nobody seems concerned about.
ai.conversationPrompt: NPCs here don't stop working to talk to you. They answer over their shoulder while pouring molten something into a mold. They're not rude — they're busy. They respect competence and materials knowledge. Ask a stupid question and you'll get a look. Ask a good one and they might show you something.

results.name: Hammer
results.description: A heavy steel hammer with a wooden handle darkened by years of use. The head is pocked with tiny craters from striking things harder than steel.
results.secret: The hammer head is made from a dimensional alloy that transfers force differently depending on what it strikes — soft on soft materials, hard on hard ones. Skilled crafters use it to shape materials that would shatter under a normal hammer.

properties.takeRefusal: The tools stay at the forge. House rules.

results.name: Tongs
results.description: Long-handled iron tongs for gripping hot materials. The tips are scarred and slightly misaligned from heavy use.
results.secret: The tongs' misalignment is intentional — the tips create a slight twist when gripping, which is the correct way to handle coppervine. Grip it straight and it fights you; grip it with a twist and it relaxes.

properties.takeRefusal: The tools stay at the forge. House rules.

results.name: Mortar and Pestle
results.description: A heavy stone mortar with a matching pestle, both worn smooth in the bowl. Traces of different colored powders stain the rim in layers.
results.secret: The layered powder residue in the mortar acts as a catalyst. Grinding a new material here mixes in trace amounts of everything previously ground, which occasionally produces unexpected results — a happy accident the regulars rely on.

properties.takeRefusal: The tools stay at the forge. House rules.

results.name: Notice Board
results.description: A weathered wooden board bristling with pinned papers. Requests for rare materials, offers of services, warnings about unreliable vendors, a few sketched maps of market sections. Most are in scripts you can't read, but some have been helpfully illustrated. One recurring notice, pinned in several places, reads: BRING FINISHED GOODS TO THE COLLECTOR — FAIR PRICES PAID.
results.secret: Some of the notices are written in a script that changes when you're not looking directly at it. One particular notice, pinned under several layers of others, is a map of the market that shows stalls and passages that don't seem to exist yet — or don't exist anymore.

scenery.description: The maps are hand-drawn on scraps of vellum and dried leaf, showing the market as a shifting cluster of circles and overlapping planes. One particular map depicts a series of back-alleys that appear to connect the nut vendor's cart directly to a distant forge. The ink on that one seems to migrate across the page if you blink too fast.
scenery.rejection: The map is pinned firmly behind several layers of more recent commerce.

properties.takeRefusal: The board is bolted to the wall.

results.name: Forge
results.description: A broad stone hearth fed by bellows that seem to pump themselves. The coals glow a steady orange-white. The heat coming off it is intense but oddly contained — step two paces back and the air is cool again.
results.secret: The forge burns fuel from a high-energy dimension — the coals don't consume, they cycle. The heat containment is a property of the fuel, not the forge design. Materials heated here briefly take on workable properties: coppervine loses its shape memory and can be bent freely, void glass becomes flexible instead of brittle, sealed clay softens without needing powder. The effects fade as things cool.

results.name: Workshop Sign
results.description: A hand-painted wooden sign nailed to a support post: USE THE TOOLS. CLEAN UP AFTER. NO FIGHTING. Below it, in smaller letters: GRIST IS ALWAYS RIGHT. Below that, in different handwriting: (GRIST DID NOT WRITE THAT.)
results.secret: The sign has been repainted dozens of times. Under the current text, earlier versions had different rules, including one that read: IF IT MOVES ON ITS OWN, PUT IT DOWN AND STEP BACK.

properties.takeRefusal: Leave the sign where it is.

results.name: Brass Scale
results.description: A small brass scale on Madame Wick's desk. One pan holds a single white feather. The other pan holds nothing. They balance perfectly.
results.secret: The scale measures perceived fairness, not weight. Place something in the empty pan and the scale tips or balances depending on whether the offered price matches the value both parties assign to the deal. It can't be fooled — it reads intent, not declared value. Madame Wick uses it to close disputes.

properties.takeRefusal: Madame Wick places one finger on the scale without looking up.

results.name: Price Board
results.description: A large slate board mounted on a wooden frame near the court entrance. Prices are chalked in neat columns. None of them are numbers. One reads: "Matched set of memories (two, related)." Another: "Four hours, uninterrupted." A third has been partially erased and rewritten several times.
results.secret: The prices update themselves. Vendors chalk their terms each morning, but the board occasionally adjusts — a price that was "one secret" yesterday might read "one secret (good)" today. Nobody admits to changing them. The partially erased entry is a price that keeps being set and then reconsidered: someone wants to sell something but can't settle on what to ask.

properties.takeRefusal: The board is bolted to its frame.

results.name: magnetic grit bin
results.description: A heavy iron bin filled with fine black sand that shivers and peaks when metal is brought near it. A small wooden scoop hangs from a chain on the side. The sand is used for polishing surfaces that are too hard for standard abrasives.
results.secret: If the grit is combined with a liquid, it creates a viscous paste that can be moved by waving a metal object over it. It can also detect hidden magnetic fields in nearby walls or floors.

results.name: pumice slurry vat
results.description: A stone basin filled with a thick, frothy gray liquid. It looks like bubbling mud, but it is actually a suspension of ultra-fine abrasive minerals used for polishing.

properties.takeRefusal: The slurry is too heavy and messy to carry without a proper container.

results.name: stuck turnstile
results.description: A heavy brass turnstile that guards the entrance to the market proper. It looks like it should rotate, but the socket for the operating lever is empty. This is apparently the market's way of ensuring only people with basic mechanical skills or a lot of patience get inside.
results.secret: The turnstile is part of a series of security measures that haven't been maintained since the last Great Reorganization. It requires a simple lever to function. Any long, sturdy rod will do, but the one in the junk pile is the most convenient.

results.name: heap of junk
results.description: A heap of discarded dimensional shipping crates and bent metal. It's the kind of pile that accumulates when nobody is quite sure if something is trash or a rare component. Someone has thoughtfully added a sign that says NOT FOR SCAVENGING, which has been largely ignored. A rusty lever pokes out from the mess.
results.secret: The pile contains a few useful bits of scrap. This is a common occurrence at the market — one person's junk is another's critical spare part.

create.name: ai-put-lever-in-turnstile
ai-put-lever-into-turnstile

input.summary: Fixed the issue where "put lever in turnstile" did not work by adding new verb handlers for "put", "insert", and "use" with various prepositions ("in", "into", "on"). Also added "socket" as an alias for the turnstile and updated its name and description once the lever is inserted to reflect its new functional state. The exit to the north is now correctly unlocked when the lever is placed.

summary: Fixed the issue where "put lever in turnstile" did not work by adding new verb handlers for "put", "insert", and "use" with various prepositions ("in", "into", "on"). Also added "socket" as an alias for the turnstile and updated its name and description once the lever is inserted to reflect its new functional state. The exit to the north is now correctly unlocked when the lever is placed.
